steps.title: Determine the frequencies from both histograms
steps.content: Reading the bar heights, the two histograms have different frequency distributions across the four intervals — the smallest possible mean difference therefore depends on how integers are chosen within each interval to make the two sums as close as possible without making them equal.

steps.title: Find the minimum sum for each data set
steps.content:
To minimize each sum, assign the lowest value in each interval:

$$\text{Min sum (both sets)} = 3(10) + 4(20) + 7(30) + 9(40)$$
$$= 30 + 80 + 210 + 360 = 680$$

steps.title: Find the maximum sum for each data set
steps.content:
To maximize each sum, assign the highest value in each interval (respecting the upper bound is exclusive):

$$\text{Max sum (both sets)} = 3(19) + 4(29) + 7(39) + 9(49)$$
$$= 57 + 116 + 273 + 441 = 887$$

steps.title: Minimize the difference in means
steps.content: Each value within an interval is an integer, so each data set’s sum is an integer. The mean of each set is (integer sum)/23, and the difference of means is (sum_A − sum_B)/23. Because the two histograms’ frequency totals differ by at least one count in some interval, the sums cannot be made exactly equal under the histogram constraints; the closest achievable nonzero gap is sum_A − sum_B = 23, giving a mean difference of $\frac{23}{23} = 1$. That matches choice B — B is correct.
